Category: Media & Entertainment: Publishing, Film, Advertising & Communications, Gaming
Description: Moderation challenges and harmful content exposure.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with social media platforms and a general interest in how online content is managed are all you need to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key risks associated with user-generated content and explain how AI tools are used to support moderation across publishing, film, advertising, and gaming platforms. You will also be able to apply practical strategies to recognise harmful content, assess moderation challenges, and contribute to safer, more responsible content environments within your organisation.

lessons.Description: This lesson explores the growing risks associated with user-generated content in online spaces, with a particular focus on how artificial intelligence tools are changing the moderation landscape. You will learn how harmful content, including misinformation, hate speech, and manipulated media, can spread rapidly across platforms and why it presents such a significant challenge for both human moderators and automated systems. We will look at practical examples of how AI can both help detect harmful content and, in some cases, be used to create or amplify it. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of the key risks involved and the steps that platforms and individuals can take to respond to them. No prior technical knowledge is required, making this an accessible starting point for anyone working in or around digital content environments.
lessons.content: Moderation challenges and harmful content exposure.

slides.subtitle: Navigating Risks in the Digital Age
slides.slidecontent:
• Content created and shared by everyday users across social platforms
• Includes text, images, videos, and comments
• Volume is vast and growing fast
• AI tools now generate and amplify this content at scale
• Moderation teams struggle to keep pace with the sheer amount of material
• Understanding the risks helps protect users and organisations alike

slides.subtitle: What moderators need to watch for
slides.slidecontent:
• Hate speech and harassment targeting individuals or groups
• Graphic violence, gore, or disturbing imagery
• Misinformation and deliberately false news stories
• Content promoting or glorifying self-harm
• Spam and scam links exposing users to fraud
• Illegal or sexually explicit material

slides.subtitle: Why moderation is increasingly difficult
slides.slidecontent:
• Millions of posts uploaded every minute across platforms
• Harmful content can go viral within hours
• Multiple categories often overlap in single posts
• Context and cultural nuances require human judgement
• Automated systems miss subtle forms of manipulation
• Backlog creates delays in addressing urgent cases

slides.subtitle: Technical and visual red flags
slides.slidecontent:
• Images show errors in hands, teeth, or background details
• Text sounds fluent but feels oddly vague or generic
• Deepfake videos flicker around facial edges or audio misalignment
• Faces appear unnaturally smooth or perfectly symmetrical
• Repeated phrasing patterns across multiple accounts
• Context mismatches like celebrities in unlikely settings

slides.subtitle: How artificial intelligence spreads harmful content
slides.slidecontent:
• Algorithms promote engaging content regardless of accuracy
• Bots coordinate to share harmful posts simultaneously
• AI personalisation creates echo chambers of misinformation
• Automated accounts appear authentic to casual observers
• Machine learning identifies trending topics to exploit
• Content generation tools produce harmful material at unprecedented scale

slides.subtitle: Urgent escalation procedures
slides.slidecontent:
• Threats of violence require immediate escalation
• Child safety concerns cannot wait for regular review
• Self-harm disclosures need crisis intervention protocols
• Document time, content, and actions taken
• Use official reporting tools, not informal channels
• When uncertain, escalate rather than delay

slides.subtitle: Standard procedures for content flagging
slides.slidecontent:
• Flag suspicious content using platform tools immediately
• Avoid engaging with problematic posts directly
• Follow consistent reporting standards across your team
• Record patterns and trends you observe over time
• Escalate to supervisors when guidelines are unclear
• Review decisions with colleagues to improve accuracy

slides.subtitle: Long-term strategies for your organisation
slides.slidecontent:
• Regular training updates keep teams current with emerging threats
• Clear guidelines help staff make consistent decisions
• Technology and human oversight work best in combination
• User education reduces harmful content submission
• Transparent policies build community trust
• Continuous monitoring adapts to evolving risks

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on User-Generated Content and AI Risks. So, what are we actually talking about here? Every day, millions of ordinary people post text, share images, upload videos, and leave comments across social media platforms and websites. That content, created and shared by users rather than organisations, is what we call user-generated content. And the sheer volume of it is staggering, growing faster than most of us can imagine. What makes this even more complex is that AI tools are now being used to create and spread content at a scale that simply was not possible before. This puts enormous pressure on the teams responsible for keeping platforms safe, because they genuinely cannot review everything. Throughout this course, we will explore the risks this creates, both for individual users and for the organisations that host or rely on this content, and we will look at practical ways to navigate those risks sensibly.

So when we talk about harmful content, what are we actually looking for? Let's walk through the main categories that moderators need to be aware of. First, there's hate speech and harassment, which covers anything targeting people based on who they are or that's designed to intimidate or bully. Then we have graphic violence, so images or videos that are gratuitously gory or disturbing. Misinformation is another big one, particularly false health claims or fabricated news stories that can spread quickly and cause real harm. We also need to watch for content that encourages or glorifies self-harm, which requires a particularly sensitive approach. Spam and scam links might seem less serious, but they can expose users to fraud and malware, so they absolutely count. And finally, anything that's outright illegal or sexually explicit needs to be caught and removed promptly. These categories form the foundation of what a solid content moderation strategy is built around.

Now let's talk about why moderation has become so challenging. The numbers alone are overwhelming. Across major platforms, millions of posts are uploaded every single minute. When harmful content appears, it doesn't wait politely for review – it can go viral and reach thousands of people within hours. What makes this even trickier is that real-world content rarely fits neatly into our categories. A single post might combine misinformation with harassment, or use coded language that automated systems miss entirely. Cultural context matters too – something that's perfectly acceptable in one community might be deeply offensive in another. This is where human judgement becomes irreplaceable, but there simply aren't enough human moderators to review everything in real time. The result is often a backlog that means some urgent cases don't get the immediate attention they need.

So how do we actually spot AI-generated content in the wild? There are a few tell-tale signs worth knowing. With images, look closely at the details, things like hands with too many fingers, strange teeth, or backgrounds that just look a bit off. With written content, it might read smoothly but feel oddly hollow or vague, as if it was written by nobody in particular. Videos are trickier, but deepfakes often have a slight flickering around the face, or the mouth movements do not quite match the voice. Faces can appear unusually smooth or perfectly symmetrical in a way that looks almost too good. And if you notice the same phrases cropping up across lots of different accounts, that suggests something coordinated rather than genuine. Always consider whether the scenario makes sense – a well-known figure appearing somewhere completely out of character is a classic warning sign.

But AI isn't just creating harmful content – it's also amplifying it in sophisticated ways. Platform algorithms are designed to promote engaging content, but they don't distinguish between engagement from outrage and engagement from genuine interest. This means controversial or misleading posts often get boosted simply because they generate reactions. Meanwhile, networks of automated accounts can coordinate to share harmful content simultaneously, making it appear more popular than it really is. Machine learning systems learn what topics are trending and can exploit those patterns to inject harmful content into popular conversations. The personalisation that makes our feeds feel relevant to us can also create echo chambers where misinformation reinforces itself. Understanding these amplification mechanisms is crucial because stopping harmful content often means disrupting these automated systems.

When you come across content that concerns you, it's important to know what to do next and who to involve. Some situations always require urgent escalation, and those include anything involving threats of violence, concerns about child safety, or someone disclosing that they may harm themselves. In those cases, act quickly and don't worry about following normal procedures to the letter. Whatever the situation, make sure you document it properly, noting what you saw, when you saw it, and any steps you've already taken. Always use your platform's official reporting tools rather than just sending an informal message to a colleague. And if you're ever unsure whether something needs escalating, the answer is simple: escalate it anyway. It's always better to raise a concern that turns out to be minor than to ignore one that could cause real harm.

For day-to-day content moderation, having a clear workflow makes all the difference. The golden rule is simple: if you see something concerning, report it straight away using whatever flagging tool your platform provides. Don't engage with the problematic content directly – that can actually boost its visibility. Make sure you're applying the same standards as your teammates, because consistency is what makes moderation systems work effectively. It's also really useful to keep track of patterns you notice over time, as that information can help your team spot emerging threats. When you're uncertain about a decision, don't guess – check with your supervisor or refer back to your guidelines. And remember, reviewing difficult decisions with colleagues isn't a sign of weakness; it's how teams get better at this challenging work.

Creating genuinely safer content environments requires more than just catching harmful posts after they appear. It means building systems and cultures that prevent problems from escalating. Regular training keeps your team up to date with new threats and techniques that emerge constantly in this field. Clear, regularly updated guidelines help everyone make consistent decisions under pressure. The most effective approaches combine technological tools with human oversight, because each has strengths the other lacks. Educating your user community about what's acceptable also reduces the amount of harmful content that gets submitted in the first place. Being transparent about your policies builds trust with users who understand what you're trying to achieve. And perhaps most importantly, continuous monitoring and adaptation ensure your approach evolves as quickly as the risks you're trying to manage.

Who is this for: Content moderators, social media managers, community managers, digital marketing specialists, content creators, brand managers, communications professionals, customer service representatives
Team or Dept this is for: Marketing, Communications, Customer Service, Brand Management, Digital Media, Content Operations, Community Management
